message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Early CoVID-19 growth obeys:
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_inline1.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  , with
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  = [(ln 2)/(
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  )], where t
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is the pandemic growth
                  <jats:italic>doubling time</jats:italic>
                  . Given
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_inline2.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  , the daily number of new CoVID-19 cases is
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_inline3.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  . Implementing society-wide
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  increases the
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>doubling time</jats:italic>
                  , and a linear function of time for
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  was used in our
                  <jats:italic>Initial Model</jats:italic>
                  :
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn1">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_ueqn1.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  to describe these changes, where the [t]-axis is time-shifted from the
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_inline4.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  –axis back to the pandemic start, and
                  <jats:italic>G</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ≡ [
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  /
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ]. While this N
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  [t] successfully modeled the USA CoVID-19 progress from 3/2020 to 6/2020, this equation could not easily model some quickly decreasing
                  <jats:italic>ρ</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] cases (“
                  <jats:italic>fast pandemic shutoff</jats:italic>
                  “), indicating that a second process was involved. This situation was most evident in the initial CoVID-19 data from
                  <jats:italic>China, South Korea</jats:italic>
                  , and
                  <jats:italic>Italy</jats:italic>
                  . Modifying
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] to allow exponential cutoffs:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn2">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20196063v1_ueqn2.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  resulted in an
                  <jats:italic>Enhanced Initial Model (EIM)</jats:italic>
                  that significantly improved data fits for these cases.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  After 6/2020, many regions of the USA “
                  <jats:italic>opened up</jats:italic>
                  ”, loosening their
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  requirements, which led to a sudden USA CoVID-19
                  <jats:italic>Resurgence</jats:italic>
                  . Extrapolating the USA
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] 3/2020-6/2020 results to 9/2020 as an
                  <jats:italic>Initial Model Baseline (IMB)</jats:italic>
                  , and subtracting this
                  <jats:italic>IMB</jats:italic>
                  from the newer USA data gives a
                  <jats:italic>Resurgence Only</jats:italic>
                  function, which is analyzed here. This USA CoVID-19
                  <jats:italic>Resurgence</jats:italic>
                  function differs significantly from the N
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  [t] IMB functional form, but it was well-modeled by the
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]
                  <jats:italic>fast pandemic shutoff</jats:italic>
                  function. These results indicate that: (a) the gradual increase in
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>doubling time</jats:italic>
                  from society-wide shut-downs is likely due to eliminating of a large number of population gathering points that could have enabled CoVID-19 spread; and (b) having a non-zero
                  <jats:italic>δ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>fast pandemic shutoff</jats:italic>
                  is likely due to more people wearing masks more often [with
                  <jats:italic>12 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ].
                </jats:p>
title: Initial Model for USA CoVID-19 Resurgence

https://www.MedRxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.06.30.20143149v1, https://doi:.org/10.1101/2020.06.30.20143149, “Orthogonal Functions Jor Evaluating Social Distancing Impact on Covid-19 Spread “, G. Eng.
https://www.MedRxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.08.07.20169904v1, https://doi:.org/10.1101/2020.08.07.20169904, “Model to Describe Fast Shutoff oJ CoVID-19 Pandemic Spread “, Genghmun Eng.
https://covid19.healthdata.org/united-states-oJ-america?view=total-deaths&tab=trend, Institute of Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), University of Washington, 9/3/2020.

Szekely ; editing by H. Goller , 9/4/2020

https://www.yahoo.com/news/coronavirus-may-last-2-years-172805061.html, “Coronavirus May Last 2 Years, Study Warns. Its 2nd wave could be Worse”, xDennis Wagner, USA Today, 5/1/20, 10:07 PM PDT.
